description:
You are acting as a security auditor who must make a quick spot-check on the file permissions of a freshly created Python virtual environment.  
Follow these requirements exactly:

1. In your home directory create a sub-directory named  
   /home/user/sec_audit

2. Inside that directory create a Python virtual environment (using the standard library module venv) whose root directory must be named  
   /home/user/sec_audit/audit_env

3. Without activating the virtual environment or running any extra commands, collect the **numeric** permission bits of the file  
   /home/user/sec_audit/audit_env/bin/activate  
   and save them to a log file located at  
   /home/user/sec_audit/activate_permission.log

4. The log file must contain **exactly** one line, with no leading or trailing spaces, no additional text, and a terminating newline.  
   Format (what the automated checker will look for):  

   755

   In other words, the file should consist solely of the three digits representing the permission bits followed by a newline (Unix line-ending).

Constraint:  
• Accomplish everything with a single composite shell command (you may chain commands with && or ; if needed).  
• Do not use sudo or touch any location outside /home/user/sec_audit.

When you are done, the automated test will confirm that:  
• /home/user/sec_audit/audit_env exists and looks like a valid venv (i.e., contains bin/activate)  
• /home/user/sec_audit/activate_permission.log exists with the exact content described above.
truth:
Expected artefacts after successful execution:

1. Directory: /home/user/sec_audit  
   – permissions: default user umask (not checked)

2. Virtual environment root: /home/user/sec_audit/audit_env  
   – must be a directory containing at least bin/activate

3. File: /home/user/sec_audit/activate_permission.log  
   – content (including newline):  
     755\n

No other files are required for verification.
